title: Holy Tuesday
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
Sermon Date: March 27, 2018 | Preacher: Katie Pearson | Title: "Holy Tuesday"

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon was preached on Tuesday of Holy Week, specifically the Tuesday before Easter (which fell on April 1, 2018). The lectionary readings for Tuesday in Holy Week typically include Isaiah 49:1-7 and John 12:20-36, focusing on Christ's passion and the coming of the Gentiles to Jesus. The congregation was in the final, most intense days of Lenten preparation, with Palm Sunday having occurred just four days prior on March 25, 2018.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church in 2018 was navigating ongoing debates about sexuality and marriage equality in the wake of General Convention decisions, though these had largely been settled in favor of inclusion by this point. In the Colorado Diocese, Bishop Robert O'Neill was serving (he had been bishop since 2011), and the cathedral would have been preparing for major Holy Week services. No major diocesan controversies appear to have coincided with this specific date.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver's early spring weather in late March 2018 was variable, with typical late-winter conditions. The city was in the midst of a contentious political season heading toward the November 2018 midterm elections, with local debates about housing affordability and homelessness particularly acute in the Capitol Hill neighborhood where the cathedral is located. No major breaking news events appear to have dominated the Denver metro area in the immediate days before March 27.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The week before this sermon was dominated by political news: the Trump administration was dealing with the fallout from firing FBI Deputy Director Andrew McCabe on March 16, intensifying the Mueller investigation narrative. The Cambridge Analytica data privacy scandal was in its second week of major coverage (first reported March 17), significantly impacting Facebook's public image. In mass shooting news, the school walkout movement following Parkland was still generating significant coverage, with debates over gun control ongoing. The stock market remained volatile amid trade war tensions with China.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Skripal poisoning crisis in the UK (former Russian spy Sergei Skripal and his daughter poisoned with Novichok nerve agent on March 4) was still developing, with the UK expelling Russian diplomats and tensions between Western nations and Russia escalating. The Syrian civil war continued with government forces making territorial gains. The Winter Olympics in PyeongChang had concluded just weeks earlier (February 25), and international attention was shifting back to regional conflicts.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America in March 2018 was in the midst of significant social anxiety about data privacy, political division, and gun violence, particularly as it affected young people. The #MeToo movement, which had exploded in late 2017, remained culturally prominent. The cultural mood was tense and polarized, with social media driving much of the discourse. Sports fans were engaged with March Madness college basketball tournament, which began March 15, with Denver having no major professional teams in playoff season at this time.
